Approach 1: National Bank of Canada Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value a bank can create above the return that equity investors require, based on its profitability and book value per share. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it starts with what the bank earns on its equity and compares that to the cost of equity.

For National Bank of Canada, the model uses a Book Value of CA$81.38 per share and a Stable EPS of CA$14.26 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 9 analysts. The Average Return on Equity used is 16.16%, while the Cost of Equity is CA$6.39 per share. That leaves an Excess Return estimate of CA$7.87 per share, which is then applied to a Stable Book Value of CA$88.23 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 8 analysts.

Putting this together, the Excess Returns model arrives at an intrinsic value of about CA$268.48 per share, which implies the stock is 33.0% undervalued relative to the current price around CA$180.

Approach 2: National Bank of Canada Price vs Earnings

P/E is a common way to value profitable banks because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is generating today. A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market is pricing in for future growth and how much risk investors see in those earnings.

National Bank of Canada currently trades on a P/E of 16.92x. That sits above the Banks industry average of 10.79x and also above the peer group average of 13.33x, which suggests the market is willing to pay more for each dollar of earnings compared with many other banks.

data.title: Some May Be Optimistic About JSL's (BVMF:JSLG3) Earnings
data.description:
Soft earnings didn't appear to concern JSL S.A.'s (BVMF:JSLG3) shareholders over the last week. Our analysis suggests that while the profits are soft, the foundations of the business are strong.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against JSL's Earnings

One key financial ratio used to measure how well a company converts its profit to free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio. In plain english, this ratio subtracts FCF from net profit, and divides that number by the company's average operating assets over that period. The ratio shows us how much a company's profit exceeds its FCF.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

JSL has an accrual ratio of -0.14 for the year to December 2025. That indicates that its free cash flow was a fair bit more than its statutory profit. In fact, it had free cash flow of R$1.1b in the last year, which was a lot more than its statutory profit of R$81.7m. Given that JSL had negative free cash flow in the prior corresponding period, the trailing twelve month resul of R$1.1b would seem to be a step in the right direction. Importantly, we note an unusual tax situation, which we discuss below, has impacted the accruals ratio.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

An Unusual Tax Situation

Moving on from the accrual ratio, we note that JSL profited from a tax benefit which contributed R$84m to profit. This is of course a bit out of the ordinary, given it is more common for companies to be paying tax than receiving tax benefits! The receipt of a tax benefit is obviously a good thing, on its own. However, our data indicates that tax benefits can temporarily boost statutory profit in the year it is booked, but subsequently profit may fall back. In the likely event the tax benefit is not repeated, we'd expect to see its statutory profit levels drop, at least in the absence of strong growth. While we think it's good that the company has booked a tax benefit, it does mean that there's every chance the statutory profit will come in a lot higher than it would be if the income was adjusted for one-off factors.

Our Take On JSL's Profit Performance

In conclusion, JSL has strong cashflow relative to earnings, which indicates good quality earnings, but the tax benefit means its profit wasn't as sustainable as we'd like to see. Considering the aforementioned, we think that JSL's profits are probably a reasonable reflection of its underlying profitability; although we'd be confident in that conclusion if we saw a cleaner set of results. If you want to do dive deeper into JSL, you'd also look into what risks it is currently facing. For instance, we've identified 4 warning signs for JSL (2 make us uncomfortable) you should be familiar with.

In this article we've looked at a number of factors that can impair the utility of profit numbers, as a guide to a business.
